semantic_journey: Arabic رصخ (rasikha) = became firm, fixed, settled solidly. Greek ἀραρίσκω = to fit together, fasten, join. Both encode the concept of securing/fixing — Arabic emphasizes the result state (solid/firm), Greek the action (join firmly). Fixation → fastening is a direct 1-step drift.
key_insight: Arabic becoming-firm and Greek fastening-together share the core of solid fixation.

semantic_journey: Λάζαρος is the Greek transliteration of the Hebrew personal name אֶלְעָזָר (Eleazar = 'God has helped'). Arabic الازر contains the name Lazarus only as a borrowing from Hebrew/Aramaic through Christian texts. This is a proper name shared via religious textual transmission, not a linguistic cognate. Excluded per proper-name rule.
key_insight: Lazarus is a Hebrew personal name (Eleazar) transmitted through Semitic religious texts, not an Arabic-Greek cognate.

semantic_journey: Arabic رسف (rasafa) = to walk in shackles/fetters, to bind the feet. Greek περισφύριον = anklet, bracelet worn around the ankle (from περί 'around' + σφυρόν 'ankle'). The Arabic root encodes ankle-binding motion; the Greek word names the object that binds the ankle. Action of fettering → the fetter ornament is a clear 1-step specialization.
key_insight: Arabic ankle-binding action specialized into the Greek word for an ankle ornament/anklet.

semantic_journey: Arabic الطسق (al-tasq) = a measure, tax, tribute paid in kind. Greek καλαθίσκος = small basket, small wicker container. The link rests only on 'container-ness' — both are vessel-like objects. But a tax measure and a small decorative basket are functionally very different, and the consonant skeletons t-s-q vs k-l-th-sk have no credible merger path. No real semantic connection.
key_insight: Tax measure and small basket share only the generic category of container; no specific semantic path exists.

semantic_journey: Arabic ذاقه (dhaqahu) / root ذوق = to taste, to take a sample of, to experience/handle. Greek δέχεται = he receives, takes. The semantic connection: tasting involves taking a portion; receiving involves taking. Arabic taking-a-taste → Greek receiving/taking is a plausible 1-step generalization from specific (taste-sampling) to general (receive/take).
key_insight: Arabic sampling/tasting generalizes into Greek receiving — both involve the act of taking in.

semantic_journey: Arabic الفن (al-fann) = a type, kind, sort; variety of expression. Greek πολυφωνία = polyphony, variety of tones/voices. While both involve variety, the Arabic word is generic (any kind) and the Greek is specifically about vocal/musical multiplicity. Furthermore, the Arabic فن may itself be borrowed from Greek or used in different contexts. The consonant skeletons f-n vs p-l-ph-n don't match well. Superficial semantic similarity, no clear cognate path.
key_insight: Generic Arabic variety (fann) and specific Greek vocal multiplicity (polyphonia) share a surface category but no direct semantic path.

semantic_journey: Arabic ساكاه (sakahu) = to press, constrict, confine someone. Greek ἐσχήκει = he had held/possessed (perfect of ἔχω). The semantic gap is large: Arabic pressing/constraining vs Greek having/possessing. While possession implies some degree of control, pressing someone and having/holding something are conceptually distinct actions requiring multiple drift steps. No direct path.
key_insight: Arabic pressing/constraining and Greek having/holding are distinct enough that no clean 2-step path exists.

semantic_journey: Arabic رفي (rafa) = to patch, repair a garment; to stitch covering material. Greek ἐρέφω = to cover with a roof, to roof over. Both encode the same conceptual act: applying material to cover and protect. Arabic covering/patching garments → Greek covering buildings with a roof is a direct 1-step parallel of the same protective-covering action at different scales.
key_insight: Arabic patching/covering garments and Greek roofing/covering buildings share a core covering-action semantic.
